description: Children use the Moveable Alphabet to build and read words with short 'i' sounds like big, sit, and hit. This reinforces phonetic awareness and early reading skills through hands-on manipulation.
instructions: Lay out work mat and arrange moveable alphabet letters by vowels and consonants
Show child the short 'i' sound by stretching it out: /i/ /i/ /i/
Select first object (pin) and sound it out slowly: /p/ /i/ /n/
Demonstrate finding each letter and placing them in order to spell 'pin'
Guide child to build 'sit' with your support, sounding out each phoneme
Allow child to independently build 'big', 'hit', and 'lid' with minimal help
Read each completed word together, running finger under letters
Match built words to corresponding picture cards for reinforcement
parent_notes: The Moveable Alphabet allows children to compose words before they can write them perfectly, separating the cognitive task from the motor skill challenge. Encourage slow, deliberate sounding out of each phoneme - this builds strong phonemic awareness. Don't worry about perfect letter formation; focus on the sounds and their sequence.

slides.heading: Building CVC Words
slides.subheading: Short 'i' Words with Moveable Alphabet
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi there! I'm Betsy Bear and today we're going to build words with our special moveable letters. We'll focus on words that have the /i/ sound in the middle, like 'sit' and 'big'!

slides.setup_instructions: Arrange letters organized by vowels and consonants on work mat, with objects and picture cards nearby
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all these wonderful letters we can use to build words! I like to keep my vowels and consonants organized so I can find them easily. These little objects will help us too!

slides.text: Introduce short 'i' sound by stretching it out and having child repeat: /i/ /i/ /i/
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's practice the sound that's in the middle of many words today! Say /i/ /i/ /i/ with me. Can you feel how your mouth makes that sound? It's right in the middle of words like 'big'!

slides.text: Demonstrate building 'pin' by sounding out /p/ /i/ /n/ and selecting each letter in sequence
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch how I listen to each sound in the word 'pin' and find the right letter for each sound. First /p/, then /i/, then /n/. Building words with letters is like solving a puzzle!

slides.prompt: Child builds 'sit', 'big', 'hit', and 'lid' using moveable alphabet with increasing independence
slides.mascot_explanation: Now you get to be the word builder! Listen carefully to each sound and find the right letters. Take your time and remember to say each sound out loud to help you.

items: Does child isolate each phoneme correctly?
Does child read completed words accurately?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're doing such amazing work with these words! I can see you're really listening to each sound and finding the perfect letters. Your reading skills are growing so strong!

slides.text: Read all completed words together and match to picture cards for final reinforcement
slides.mastery_check: Child successfully builds and reads at least 3 short 'i' CVC words independently
slides.mascot_explanation: What wonderful words you've built today! Let's read them all together one more time and match them to their pictures. You should feel so proud of being such a great word builder!

title: Hundred Board: Finding Number Patterns by Tens
description: Children explore patterns on the Hundred Board by identifying and continuing sequences that increase by ten. This builds understanding of place value and base-ten relationships.
instructions: Set up hundred board with several number tiles already placed in correct positions
Point out the pattern when moving down one row: 5, 15, 25, 35...
Have child place colored bears on numbers that follow this pattern
Demonstrate how each number increases by exactly ten when going down
Remove some tiles and have child predict what numbers belong there
Practice with different starting numbers: 3, 13, 23, 33...
Complete partial sequences on worksheet using discovered patterns
Discuss how the ones place stays the same while tens place increases
parent_notes: The Hundred Board is a powerful tool for visualizing number relationships and patterns in our base-ten system. Allow children to discover patterns through manipulation rather than telling them directly. This concrete experience with abstract concepts builds deep mathematical understanding that will support future work with place value and operations.

slides.heading: Hundred Board
slides.subheading: Finding Number Patterns by Tens
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello mathematicians! I'm Betsy Bear and today we're going to be number detectives with our special Hundred Board. We're going to discover some amazing patterns when we count by tens!

slides.setup_instructions: Place hundred board on table with number tiles organized nearby and counting bears in small bowl
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at our wonderful hundred board! It has spaces for all the numbers from 1 to 100. We'll use these number tiles and colorful bears to discover some really cool patterns today!

slides.text: Demonstrate pattern by placing tiles vertically: 5, 15, 25, 35 and point out the increase by ten
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch what happens when I put numbers going down like this: 5, 15, 25, 35! Do you notice something special about these numbers? Each one is exactly 10 more than the one above it!

slides.text: Have child mark pattern with colored bears and predict next numbers in sequence
slides.mascot_explanation: Now you can put a bear on each number that follows our pattern! Can you guess what number comes next? You're becoming such a good pattern detective!

slides.prompt: Child practices finding and continuing different 'by tens' patterns starting with various numbers (3, 7, 9)
slides.mascot_explanation: Now let's try making patterns starting with different numbers! What happens if we start with 3 and go down? Or 7? You get to be the pattern maker now!

items: Can child identify the +10 pattern when moving down rows?
Does child predict missing numbers correctly?
Can child explain why ones place stays the same?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're doing such incredible work finding these patterns! I can see you understand how the numbers grow by 10 each time. Your math brain is getting so strong!

slides.text: Review discovered patterns and discuss how ones place stays constant while tens place increases
slides.mastery_check: Child successfully identifies and continues at least 2 different 'by tens' patterns on the hundred board
slides.mascot_explanation: What amazing patterns we discovered today! You learned that when we go down on our board, we always add 10, and the ones number stays the same. You're such a wonderful mathematician!

title: Montessori Bells: Matching Identical Pitch Pairs
description: Children develop auditory discrimination by matching pairs of Montessori bells that produce identical pitches. This foundational musical activity refines the sense of hearing and introduces musical concepts.
materials_needed: 4 pairs of matching bells (C, D, E, F)
instructions: Arrange bells randomly on work mat, keeping one set as controls
Demonstrate proper striking technique with gentle mallet tap
Strike first control bell and listen carefully to its pitch
Test bells from second set one by one to find the match
When match is found, place bells side by side
Have child find matches for remaining three pairs
Practice striking matched pairs together to confirm identical sound
Arrange matched pairs in order using control chart reference
parent_notes: The Montessori bells develop musical ear and auditory discrimination through concrete experience. This is preparation for future musical study and refines one of the child's senses. Work in a quiet environment and emphasize careful listening rather than speed. Some children may need multiple sessions to distinguish subtle pitch differences.

slides.heading: Montessori Bells
slides.subheading: Matching Identical Pitch Pairs
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello music lovers! I'm Betsy Bear and today we're going to work with beautiful bells that make musical sounds. We'll use our ears very carefully to find bells that sound exactly the same!

items: 4 pairs of matching bells (C, D, E, F)
slides.setup_instructions: Place one set of bells in order as controls, arrange second set randomly on work mat with mallets nearby
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at these beautiful bells! Each one makes a different musical sound, and somewhere there's another bell that sounds exactly the same. We'll use these special mallets to make gentle, lovely sounds.

slides.text: Demonstrate proper bell striking technique with gentle tap, listening carefully to the resulting pitch
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch how I strike the bell very gently to make a clear, beautiful sound. We want to listen very carefully to each note. Gentle taps make the prettiest sounds!

slides.text: Model matching process by striking control bell, then testing each bell in second set until match is found
slides.mascot_explanation: Now I'll be a sound detective! First I listen to this bell, then I test the other bells one by one until I find the one that sounds exactly the same. Listen carefully with me!

slides.prompt: Child finds matches for remaining bell pairs using careful listening and systematic testing
slides.mascot_explanation: Now you get to be the sound detective! Take your time and listen very carefully to each bell. Trust your ears - they're getting very good at hearing the differences and similarities!

items: Does child strike bells with appropriate gentle technique?
Can child identify when pitches match?
Does child work systematically through the bells?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're doing such careful listening work! I can see you're using your ears so well to find the matching sounds. Your musical sense is growing beautifully!

slides.text: Strike all matched pairs together and arrange in order using control chart
slides.mastery_check: Child successfully matches at least 3 of 4 bell pairs through auditory discrimination
slides.mascot_explanation: What beautiful music we made today! You found so many matching pairs with your careful listening. Let's play them all together to hear our lovely musical scale!

title: Chrysanthemum by Kevin Henkes: Celebrating Name Uniqueness
description: Read aloud Kevin Henkes' story about a mouse who learns to love her unique name despite teasing from classmates. Discuss themes of self-acceptance and the special meaning behind names.
materials_needed: name cards for each family member
instructions: Show book cover and have child predict what story might be about
Read story with expression, pausing for child reactions and questions
Discuss how Chrysanthemum felt when others teased her name
Talk about what makes each person's name special and unique
Create name cards for each family member with decorative elements
Practice saying each family member's full name with pride
Share the story behind child's name and its special meaning
Look in mirror together while saying 'I love my name because...'
parent_notes: This story provides an excellent opportunity to discuss self-acceptance and uniqueness in an age-appropriate way. Use the book to celebrate what makes each child special, including their name. Children this age are developing their identity and may sometimes feel different or worry about fitting in, making this message particularly relevant.

slides.heading: Chrysanthemum
slides.subheading: Celebrating Name Uniqueness
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello dear friends! I'm Betsy Bear and today we're reading about a little mouse with a very special name - Chrysanthemum! This story will help us think about how wonderful and unique all our names are.

items: name cards for each family member
slides.setup_instructions: Gather in comfortable reading spot with book and art supplies nearby, have mirror accessible for activity
slides.mascot_explanation: We have everything ready for our special story time! After we read about Chrysanthemum, we'll make beautiful name cards and talk about what makes each of our names so special and wonderful.

slides.text: Show book cover and encourage child to make predictions about the story content
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at this sweet little mouse on the cover! Her name is Chrysanthemum - that's a long, beautiful name like a flower. What do you think might happen in her story?

slides.text: Read story expressively, pausing for reactions and discussing Chrysanthemum's feelings throughout
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's read together and pay attention to how Chrysanthemum feels about her name. Sometimes she feels happy about it, and sometimes she feels sad. We can understand those feelings too!

slides.prompt: Create decorated name cards for family members while discussing what makes each name special and meaningful
slides.mascot_explanation: Now we get to celebrate all the special names in your family! Let's make beautiful cards for everyone and talk about why each name is wonderful and unique, just like Chrysanthemum's name.

items: How did Chrysanthemum feel about her name at different parts of the story?
What made her feel better about her name?
slides.mascot_explanation: You understood this story so well! You could see how Chrysanthemum's feelings changed and what helped her feel proud of her beautiful name again. That shows you're a very thoughtful listener!

slides.text: Look in mirror together while sharing positive thoughts about own name and its special meaning
slides.mastery_check: Child expresses understanding that names are special and unique, like Chrysanthemum's
slides.mascot_explanation: Just like Chrysanthemum learned to love her name, we can feel proud of our own special names too! Look in the mirror and say something wonderful about your name - you're absolutely perfect just as you are!
